verses.text: And it came to pass, when the LORD would take up Elijah into heaven by a whirlwind, that Elijah went with Elisha from Gilgal. And Elijah said unto Elisha, Tarry here, I pray thee; for the LORD hath sent me to Bethel. And Elisha said unto him, As the LORD liveth, and as thy soul liveth, I will not leave thee. So they went down to Bethel. Guidance: The chapter opens with the most extraordinary preview-headline in the prophetic narratives: 'When the LORD would take up Elijah into heaven by a whirlwind.' Hebrew be-ha'alot YHWH et-eliyahu ba-se'arah ha-shamayim — 'when the LORD took up Elijah in the whirlwind to the heavens.' The narrator tells us the ending before the story begins. Elijah is one of only two people in the Old Testament to depart this life without dying (Enoch is the other — Gen 5:24). The whirlwind (se'arah) is the same word used in Job 38:1 for the storm from which the LORD answered Job; it is the storm-vehicle of divine appearing. Elijah's departure will not be a death but a translation — a direct transport from earth to the LORD's presence. The action begins at Gilgal — a different Gilgal from the one near Jericho; this is probably a hill-country Gilgal near Bethel. Elijah and Elisha set out together. Elijah's first attempt to part from his successor: 'Tarry here, I pray thee; for the LORD hath sent me to Bethel.' The chapter establishes a three-fold pattern: Gilgal → Bethel → Jericho → Jordan, with Elijah at each stop asking Elisha to remain and Elisha refusing. The pattern serves at least two purposes. (1) It tests Elisha's commitment. If he wants the double portion, he must want it enough to refuse three opportunities to step away from the cost. (2) It walks them through the major prophetic centers of the kingdom — Gilgal, Bethel, Jericho — locations associated with the schools of the prophets (benei ha-nevi'im). Elijah is bidding farewell to the prophetic community he has shepherded. Elisha's response is one of the great loyalty-oaths of Scripture: 'As the LORD liveth, and as thy soul liveth, I will not leave thee.' Hebrew chai-YHWH ve-chei nafshekha im-e'ezbekha — 'as the LORD lives and as your soul lives, [may I be cursed] if I leave you.' A double oath — the LORD's life and Elijah's life — bound by the formula im (literally 'if,' an unfinished curse formula). Elisha refuses to be separated. The chapter is showing us the disciple's full commitment. Elisha was not following a comfortable mentor; he was following a man going into death-by-whirlwind. He could have accepted any of three offered exits. He refused all three. The chapter is teaching us a foundational principle of spiritual succession: the next generation receives the mantle only when it refuses every offered shortcut. Elisha must follow his master to the end of his ministry; only at the literal moment of departure will the double portion become available. Faithfulness is the visible mark of those who will inherit.

And it came to pass, when the LORD would take up Elijah into heaven by a whirlwind, that Elijah went with Elisha from Gilgal. And Elijah said unto Elisha, Tarry here, I pray thee; for the LORD hath sent me to Bethel. And Elisha said unto him, As the LORD liveth, and as thy soul liveth, I will not leave thee. So they went down to Bethel. Guidance: The chapter opens with the most extraordinary preview-headline in the prophetic narratives: 'When the LORD would take up Elijah into heaven by a whirlwind.' A double oath — the LORD's life and Elijah's life — bound by the formula im (literally 'if,' an unfinished curse formula). Elisha refuses to be separated. The chapter is showing us the disciple's full commitment. Elisha was not following a comfortable mentor; he was following a man going into death-by-whirlwind. He could have accepted any of three offered exits. He refused all three. The chapter is teaching us a foundational principle of spiritual succession: the next generation receives the mantle only when it refuses every offered shortcut. Elisha must follow his master to the end of his ministry; only at the literal moment of departure will the double portion become available. Faithfulness is the visible mark of those who will inherit.

And the sons of the prophets that were at Bethel came forth to Elisha, and said unto him, Knowest thou that the LORD will take away thy master from thy head to day? And he said, Yea, I know it; hold ye your peace. And Elijah said unto him, Elisha, tarry here, I pray thee; for the LORD hath sent me to Jericho. And he said, As the LORD liveth, and as thy soul liveth, I will not leave thee. So they came to Jericho. Guidance: The Bethel encounter and the Jericho leg. The 'sons of the prophets' (benei ha-nevi'im) — the prophetic guild members at Bethel — come out to meet Elisha. Their question reveals that the day's events are widely known among the prophets: 'Knowest thou that the LORD will take away thy master from thy head to day?' Hebrew yodea ki ha-yom YHWH lokach et-adonekha me-al roshekha — 'do you know that today the LORD will take your master from above your head?' The phrase 'from thy head' implies the master-disciple relationship; Elisha has been under Elijah's authority, and that authority is being lifted. Elisha's response is clipped: 'Yea, I know it; hold ye your peace.' Hebrew gam-ani yadati hechshu — 'I too know; be silent.' He does not want the chatter. The pending event is sacred and personal; he refuses to make conversation about it. The chapter is showing us the disciple's restraint. There is a kind of spiritual sharing that is appropriate and a kind that is voyeurism. Elisha knows what is about to happen; he refuses to let the news circulate as topic-of-the-day. Some things deserve silence. Then the second invitation to part: 'Elisha, tarry here, I pray thee; for the LORD hath sent me to Jericho.' Same form as the first request. Same response from Elisha: 'As the LORD liveth, and as thy soul liveth, I will not leave thee.' The pair set out to Jericho. The chapter is showing us a pattern of disciplined repetition. Each opportunity Elisha refuses is a deliberate act of will. He is not coasting on emotion; he is choosing, each time, to refuse the offered exit. The chapter is teaching us that loyalty is not a state; it is a recurring decision. Each time the master offers the disciple a chance to step away, the disciple must choose again.

And the sons of the prophets that were at Bethel came forth to Elisha, and said unto him, Knowest thou that the LORD will take away thy master from thy head to day? And he said, Yea, I know it; hold ye your peace. And Elijah said unto him, Elisha, tarry here, I pray thee; for the LORD hath sent me to Jericho. And he said, As the LORD liveth, and as thy soul liveth, I will not leave thee. So they came to Jericho. Guidance: The Bethel encounter and the Jericho leg. And the sons of the prophets that were at Jericho came to Elisha, and said unto him, Knowest thou that the LORD will take away thy master from thy head to day? And he answered, Yea, I know it; hold ye your peace. And Elijah said unto him, Tarry, I pray thee, here; for the LORD hath sent me to Jordan. And he said, As the LORD liveth, and as thy soul liveth, I will not leave thee. And they two went on. Guidance: The Jericho encounter and the Jordan leg. The Jericho sons of the prophets repeat the Bethel pattern. They come out to meet Elisha; they ask the same loaded question; they receive the same restraining answer. The chapter is showing us the unity of the prophetic schools. Bethel and Jericho both know what is about to happen; the LORD has revealed the impending departure to His prophetic community widely. The information was not hidden; it was simply not for general conversation. Elijah's third request to part: 'Tarry, I pray thee, here; for the LORD hath sent me to Jordan.' The next leg is to the Jordan River — the boundary of the land, the place of Moses' last sight of Canaan (Deut 34), the place of Joshua's miraculous crossing (Josh 3-4). The location is loaded with meaning. Elisha's third refusal: 'As the LORD liveth, and as thy soul liveth, I will not leave thee.' Identical formula. Then the moving phrase: 'And they two went on.' Hebrew vayelechu sheneihem — 'the two of them walked.' The narrative pauses on the image of master and disciple walking together toward the Jordan. The chapter is closing in on the climactic moment. The crowd at the various prophetic schools is left behind; only the two of them are walking now. The chapter is teaching us that the most important spiritual moments often unfold without crowd attendance. The whirlwind will not be public theater; it will be witnessed by one disciple alone. Faithfulness purchases the privilege of solitary witness.

And the sons of the prophets that were at Jericho came to Elisha, and said unto him, Knowest thou that the LORD will take away thy master from thy head to day? And he answered, Yea, I know it; hold ye your peace. And Elijah said unto him, Tarry, I pray thee, here; for the LORD hath sent me to Jordan. And he said, As the LORD liveth, and as thy soul liveth, I will not leave thee. And they two went on. Guidance: The Jericho encounter and the Jordan leg. And fifty men of the sons of the prophets went, and stood to view afar off: and they two stood by Jordan. And Elijah took his mantle, and wrapped it together, and smote the waters, and they were divided hither and thither, so that they two went over on dry ground. Guidance: The Jordan parted. Fifty men from the Jericho prophetic school follow at a distance — they want to witness the event but are kept far back. Elijah and Elisha alone stand by the Jordan. Elijah takes off his mantle (Hebrew adderet — the hairy cloak of v8 / 1 Kgs 19:13, the same garment Elisha will inherit). He 'wrapped it together' — Hebrew vayyiglom — rolled it into a tight bundle. Then he 'smote the waters.' The act is deliberately Mosaic. Moses raised his staff over the Sea of Reeds and the waters divided (Exod 14:21); Joshua's priests stepped into the Jordan and it parted (Josh 3:13-17); now Elijah uses the rolled mantle as a striking-instrument and the Jordan divides 'hither and thither' (henah va-henah — in two directions). The dry ground (be-charavah) is the same dry-ground language as Moses' and Joshua's miracles. The chapter is teaching us that Elijah's ministry is being framed deliberately in continuity with Moses and Joshua. The same divine power that brought Israel out of Egypt and into Canaan is now demonstrating that Elijah's mantle carries Moses' authority. The chapter is also preparing us for what follows. The mantle struck the Jordan and the Jordan parted. The same mantle, in a few verses, will fall to Elisha and Elisha will strike the Jordan and the Jordan will part. The mantle is being made visible as the instrument of the parting; when it passes to Elisha, the question will be whether the LORD parts waters for the mantle or for the man.

And fifty men of the sons of the prophets went, and stood to view afar off: and they two stood by Jordan. And Elijah took his mantle, and wrapped it together, and smote the waters, and they were divided hither and thither, so that they two went over on dry ground. Guidance: The Jordan parted. Fifty men from the Jericho prophetic school follow at a distance — they want to witness the event but are kept far back. Elijah and Elisha alone stand by the Jordan. Then he 'smote the waters.' The act is deliberately Mosaic. Moses raised his staff over the Sea of Reeds and the waters divided (Exod 14:21); Joshua's priests stepped into the Jordan and it parted (Josh 3:13-17); now Elijah uses the rolled mantle as a striking-instrument and the Jordan divides 'hither and thither' (henah va-henah — in two directions). The chapter is teaching us that Elijah's ministry is being framed deliberately in continuity with Moses and Joshua. And it came to pass, when they were gone over, that Elijah said unto Elisha, Ask what I shall do for thee, before I be taken away from thee. And Elisha said, I pray thee, let a double portion of thy spirit be upon me. And he said, Thou hast asked a hard thing: nevertheless, if thou see me when I am taken from thee, it shall be so unto thee; but if not, it shall not be so. Guidance: The double-portion request. Now safely on the east side of the Jordan — outside the land of Israel, in the same area where Moses had ended his ministry — Elijah turns to his disciple: 'Ask what I shall do for thee, before I be taken away from thee.' The Hebrew she'al mah e'eseh-lekha — 'ask what I should do for you.' Elijah offers his disciple a final benediction. Elisha's request is one of the most consequential prayers in the prophetic books: 'I pray thee, let a double portion of thy spirit be upon me.' Hebrew vihi-na pi shenayim be-ruchakha elai — 'let a mouth/portion of two in your spirit be upon me.' The phrase 'double portion' (pi shenayim) is the technical term from Deut 21:17 for the firstborn's inheritance share. Under Israelite inheritance law, the firstborn received twice as much as any other son. Elisha is asking, in effect, for the firstborn's share of Elijah's prophetic spirit. He is not asking to be twice as great as Elijah; he is asking to inherit as the principal heir among Elijah's disciples — to be the prophetic firstborn. (The fulfillment will indeed produce roughly twice as many recorded miracles in Elisha's ministry as in Elijah's; some commentators count 16 Elisha miracles to 8 Elijah miracles. But this is consequence, not the request's literal content. Elisha asked for the heir's share, and the LORD gave it with the heir's full inheritance.) Elijah's response: 'Thou hast asked a hard thing.' Hebrew hiqshita lish'ol — 'you have hardened to ask' — you have asked something difficult. Not Elijah's to give — the Spirit's distribution belongs to the LORD alone. But Elijah identifies a sign-marker: 'If thou see me when I am taken from thee, it shall be so unto thee; but if not, it shall not be so.' If Elisha is permitted to witness the actual departure — the whirlwind, the chariot — that visual witness will be the LORD's signal that the double portion is granted. The chapter is teaching us that great spiritual requests require great spiritual attentiveness. Elisha must keep his eyes open through the most dazzling event he will ever see; the request is granted to the disciple who refuses to look away from the master's departure. The chapter is also showing us that Elijah does not promise what is not his to promise. He cannot guarantee the double portion; he can only identify the sign by which Elisha will know it has been given.

And it came to pass, as they still went on, and talked, that, behold, there appeared a chariot of fire, and horses of fire, and parted them both asunder; and Elijah went up by a whirlwind into heaven. And Elisha saw it, and he cried, My father, my father, the chariot of Israel, and the horsemen thereof. And he saw him no more: and he took hold of his own clothes, and rent them in two pieces. Guidance: The ascension and the cry. The two are walking — 'as they still went on, and talked' — when the moment arrives. The narrator gives the vision in three vivid components. 'A chariot of fire' (rekhev esh) — a flaming war-chariot. 'Horses of fire' (susei esh) — flaming horses. The fire-chariot 'parted them both asunder' — physically separated master from disciple. 'And Elijah went up by a whirlwind into heaven.' Hebrew vayya'al eliyahu ba-se'arah ha-shamayim. The whirlwind carries him up. The chapter is describing a theophany — the LORD's appearing in fire-and-storm. The chariot-and-horses imagery has both an immediate and a symbolic function. Immediately, they are the angelic transport that carries Elijah away. Symbolically, they are the LORD's army — His armies of fire stand around His people; Elijah's ministry has been like a divine cavalry unit operating in Israel. Elisha's cry captures both meanings. 'My father, my father' (avi avi) — the disciple-as-son addressing the master-as-father. The relationship has been deeply personal; the disciple loses his spiritual father. Then: 'The chariot of Israel, and the horsemen thereof' (rekhev yisrael u-pharashav). Elijah was Israel's protective cavalry. The prophet's intercession and prophetic confrontation had been Israel's actual defense — far more than any king's army. With Elijah gone, the visible defense of Israel disappears. (The same phrase will be applied to Elisha at his death — 2 Kgs 13:14 — by King Joash; the title applies to true prophets generally, but Elijah is the first to receive it.) 'And he saw him no more' — the seeing ends. The condition for the double portion is met — Elisha did see; his eyes were open through the entire event. Then the grief response: 'He took hold of his own clothes, and rent them in two pieces.' The traditional Israelite gesture of mourning — tearing one's own garments. Elisha grieves the loss of his master even while he has received the sign of inheritance. The chapter is showing us that spiritual succession does not eliminate grief. Elisha will become the new prophet; he is also losing the one who shaped him. The chapter is teaching us that promotion to greater responsibility does not erase the cost of those who carried the responsibility before. Elisha tears his clothes because his father is gone, even though his father has not died.

He took up also the mantle of Elijah that fell from him, and went back, and stood by the bank of Jordan; And he took the mantle of Elijah that fell from him, and smote the waters, and said, Where is the LORD God of Elijah? and when he also had smitten the waters, they parted hither and thither: and Elisha went over. Guidance: The mantle and the parting. The whirlwind has taken Elijah but left behind his mantle — the hairy cloak that had been the prophet's signature garment. It has 'fallen from him' (Hebrew nafelah me'alav) — perhaps deliberately dropped by Elijah as last gift, perhaps shaken loose by the whirlwind's force. Either way, it lies on the ground. Elisha picks it up. The act is loaded with meaning. The mantle had been the symbol of Elijah's commissioning of Elisha back in 1 Kgs 19:19 (when Elijah had cast it over the plowing Elisha). It is now Elisha's by inheritance. Elisha 'went back, and stood by the bank of Jordan.' He retraces his steps. The Jordan has closed behind them after Elijah's parting; Elisha now faces the same river that has just been miraculously crossed. The question is whether the miracle was Elijah's or the LORD's. Elisha takes the mantle and smites the waters — the same action Elijah performed (v8). His words as he strikes: 'Where is the LORD God of Elijah?' Hebrew ayyeh YHWH elohei eliyahu — 'where is the LORD, the God of Elijah?' The question is not a complaint; it is an invocation. Elisha is calling on the same LORD who had parted the Jordan for Elijah to part it for him. The question structure invokes precedent: this God did this for Elijah; will He do it for me? The waters part. 'And Elisha went over.' The chapter is showing us the principle of succession verified by power. Elijah's God had parted the Jordan; if Elisha is genuinely Elijah's prophetic heir, the same God will do the same act through him. The parting confirms the inheritance. The chapter is teaching us that authentic succession is not paperwork; it is the same divine power operating through the new instrument. The mantle is the visible sign; the parted river is the divine confirmation. Elisha is now the LORD's prophet to Israel, with the firstborn's portion of Elijah's spirit.

He took up also the mantle of Elijah that fell from him, and went back, and stood by the bank of Jordan; And he took the mantle of Elijah that fell from him, and smote the waters, and said, Where is the LORD God of Elijah? and when he also had smitten the waters, they parted hither and thither: and Elisha went over. Guidance: The mantle and the parting. The whirlwind has taken Elijah but left behind his mantle — the hairy cloak that had been the prophet's signature garment. Elisha picks it up. The act is loaded with meaning. The mantle had been the symbol of Elijah's commissioning of Elisha back in 1 Kgs 19:19 (when Elijah had cast it over the plowing Elisha). It is now Elisha's by inheritance. Elisha 'went back, and stood by the bank of Jordan.' He retraces his steps. The Jordan has closed behind them after Elijah's parting; Elisha now faces the same river that has just been miraculously crossed. The question is whether the miracle was Elijah's or the LORD's. Elisha takes the mantle and smites the waters — the same action Elijah performed (v8). His words as he strikes: 'Where is the LORD God of Elijah?' Hebrew ayyeh YHWH elohei eliyahu — 'where is the LORD, the God of Elijah?' The question is not a complaint; it is an invocation. Elisha is calling on the same LORD who had parted the Jordan for Elijah to part it for him. The question structure invokes precedent: this God did this for Elijah; will He do it for me? The waters part. 'And Elisha went over.' The chapter is showing us the principle of succession verified by power. Elijah's God had parted the Jordan; if Elisha is genuinely Elijah's prophetic heir, the same God will do the same act through him. The parting confirms the inheritance. The chapter is teaching us that authentic succession is not paperwork; it is the same divine power operating through the new instrument. The mantle is the visible sign; the parted river is the divine confirmation. Elisha is now the LORD's prophet to Israel, with the firstborn's portion of Elijah's spirit.

And when the sons of the prophets which were to view at Jericho saw him, they said, The spirit of Elijah doth rest on Elisha. And they came to meet him, and bowed themselves to the ground before him. Guidance: The recognition by the prophetic community. The 50 men of the prophetic school, watching from a distance, see Elisha return alone — but with Elijah's mantle and with a Jordan parted in front of him. They draw the obvious conclusion: 'The spirit of Elijah doth rest on Elisha.' Hebrew nachah ruach eliyahu al-elisha — 'the spirit of Elijah has rested upon Elisha.' The chapter uses the verb nuach — 'rest' — the same verb used of the Spirit's resting on various prophets and judges. The community recognizes the transfer. They come to meet him and bow themselves to the ground. The act of bowing is the social acknowledgment of his new authority. He is no longer Elijah's servant; he is Elijah's successor — the LORD's prophet to Israel, the one through whom the divine word will now come. The chapter is showing us that authentic spiritual authority is recognized by those positioned to see it. The 50 had been at Jericho watching; they saw Elisha walk back through the parted Jordan; they immediately understood what had happened. The chapter is teaching us that the prophetic community's role includes the recognition of who carries the prophetic gift. Elisha did not have to demand his title; the community gave it to him because the LORD had given it to him. The chapter is also showing us a healthy contrast with the 400 prophets of 1 Kgs 22. Those prophets had no sense of who was authentic and who was not; these 50 instantly recognize the genuine. The difference is in their orientation: the 400 sought royal patronage; these 50 sought the LORD's voice. They could tell the difference because they were looking for the right thing.

And they said unto him, Behold now, there be with thy servants fifty strong men; let them go, we pray thee, and seek thy master: lest peradventure the Spirit of the LORD hath taken him up, and cast him upon some mountain, or into some valley. And he said, Ye shall not send. And when they urged him till he was ashamed, he said, Send. They sent therefore fifty men; and they sought three days, but found him not. And when they came again to him, (for he tarried at Jericho,) he said unto them, Did I not say unto you, Go not? Guidance: The futile search for Elijah. The 50 men of the prophetic school, even after acknowledging Elisha's authority, propose a search party. 'There be with thy servants fifty strong men; let them go, we pray thee, and seek thy master: lest peradventure the Spirit of the LORD hath taken him up, and cast him upon some mountain, or into some valley.' Their theory is that the Spirit transported Elijah but then perhaps deposited him in some remote location where he might still be found. They want to organize a search. Elisha refuses: 'Ye shall not send.' The Hebrew lo-tishlachu — 'you shall not send.' He knows where Elijah is — taken to the LORD's presence, not deposited on a mountainside. There is no body to find. But the 50 press him. 'When they urged him till he was ashamed, he said, Send.' Hebrew vayyiph'tzeru-vo ad-bosh — 'until he was ashamed [to refuse further].' Social pressure overrides Elisha's prophetic certainty. He gives in. The 50 search for three days. They find nothing. They return. Elisha's response: 'Did I not say unto you, Go not?' The chapter is showing us a striking detail of the new prophet's character. He yields to pressure when he should not have. He told them the truth first; they pressured him; he allowed the futile search even though he knew it was futile. The chapter is teaching us a humbling lesson about even great prophets. Elisha is not yet at full strength of leadership. He will grow into the role; but at this stage, the community's social pressure can still bend his judgment. The chapter is also showing us the difficulty of accepting Elijah's translation. The 50 want to find a body; they want a tomb; they want closure. The reality — that the LORD has simply taken Elijah away — is theologically harder to absorb than a recoverable corpse would have been. The chapter is teaching us that some divine acts resist our need for tidy endings. We have to let them stand mysterious rather than search for explanations that fit our categories.

And the men of the city said unto Elisha, Behold, I pray thee, the situation of this city is pleasant, as my lord seeth: but the water is naught, and the ground barren. And he said, Bring me a new cruse, and put salt therein. And they brought it to him. And he went forth unto the spring of the waters, and cast the salt in there, and said, Thus saith the LORD, I have healed these waters; there shall not be from thence any more death or barren land. So the waters were healed unto this day, according to the saying of Elisha which he spake. Guidance: The Jericho water healed — Elisha's first public miracle. The men of Jericho approach the new prophet: 'The situation of this city is pleasant... but the water is naught.' Hebrew hineh-na moshav ha-ir tov... ve-ha-mayim ra'im. The city's location is excellent (Jericho is famously well-positioned in a fertile oasis near major trade routes), but the spring water is 'bad' (ra'im — evil, defective). 'The ground barren' — Hebrew vehaaretz meshakkalet — the land is causing miscarriages (the same root used of land-induced miscarriage in livestock and crops). The water is causing infertility — either through chemical contamination or through some divinely-imposed curse (perhaps related to Joshua's ancient curse on Jericho-rebuilders in Josh 6:26 / 1 Kgs 16:34). Elisha's solution: 'Bring me a new cruse, and put salt therein.' A new vessel (tzelochit chadashah) — ritually unused; and salt (melach). Salt in the ancient world was a preservative and a covenant-symbol (the 'salt of the covenant' in Lev 2:13; Num 18:19). Elisha goes to the spring (motza ha-mayim — 'source of the waters' — the actual spring, not just a downstream sample) and throws the salt in. His proclamation: 'Thus saith the LORD, I have healed these waters; there shall not be from thence any more death or barren land.' The healing is from the LORD (rapithi — 'I have healed,' first person, the LORD speaking through Elisha). 'So the waters were healed unto this day' — the narrator notes the miracle's permanence. The spring's healing persists to the chronicler's day (the modern Ein-es-Sultan spring near Jericho, traditionally identified as Elisha's spring, still provides good water for Jericho's farms). The chapter is showing us Elisha's ministry beginning with restoration. Elijah's first major public act in Israel was withholding rain (1 Kgs 17:1) — a judgment ministry. Elisha's first major public act is healing water — a restoration ministry. The two prophets have complementary emphases. Elijah confronted Israel with crisis; Elisha mostly nurses Israel through the consequences. The chapter is teaching us a leadership succession principle. The successor often serves a different season than the predecessor. Elijah's job was confrontation; Elisha's job will be cultivation. Both are the LORD's work; they meet different needs in different seasons.

And he went up from thence unto Bethel: and as he was going up by the way, there came forth little children out of the city, and mocked him, and said unto him, Go up, thou bald head; go up, thou bald head. And he turned back, and looked on them, and cursed them in the name of the LORD. And there came forth two she bears out of the wood, and tare forty and two children of them. And he went from thence to mount Carmel, and from thence he returned to Samaria. Guidance: The Bethel mockers and the bears — one of the most difficult passages in the prophetic books. As Elisha travels from Jericho to Bethel — the same Bethel where the prophetic school had been, but also the site of Jeroboam's calf-altar (1 Kgs 12:28-29) — he is met by 'little children' (Hebrew ne'arim ketannim) on the road. Their mockery: 'Go up, thou bald head; go up, thou bald head.' Hebrew aleh qereach aleh qereach. Several details require careful reading. (1) 'Little children' (ne'arim ketannim): the word ne'arim covers a wide age range — from infants through young men. The qualifier ketannim ('small') suggests they were not infants; the typical range is best understood as adolescent young men, perhaps in their teens. (2) 'Bald head' (qereach): Elisha was likely either prematurely bald or shaved his head as a sign of prophetic mourning for Elijah; baldness in Hebrew culture sometimes carried connotations of mourning or leprosy. (3) 'Go up' (aleh): the same verb used of Elijah's ascension. The mockery is not random; it appears to be a public mocking of Elijah's translation: 'Go up, like your master did. Go on, disappear, baldy.' The taunt is targeted theological mockery, not childish teasing. (4) The location matters: Bethel was the headquarters of the calf-cult; its young men were the rising generation of Israel's apostate establishment. Elisha 'turned back, and looked on them, and cursed them in the name of the LORD.' Hebrew vayyaqallelem be-shem YHWH — pronounced a curse-formula in the LORD's name. The curse is not Elisha's personal anger; it is invocation of covenantal sanctions (Lev 26 / Deut 28 had specified wild-beast attack as one of the covenantal curses on Israel's apostasy). The consequence: 'There came forth two she bears out of the wood, and tare forty and two children of them.' Two female bears (dubbim — bears, the feminine plural; she-bears are particularly dangerous because of cub-defense instinct) attack the group. 42 of the young men are 'torn' (Hebrew tibqa'na — split, ripped). The text does not specify deaths; the verb suggests serious mauling but not necessarily kills. The chapter is teaching us multiple hard lessons. (1) Mocking a true prophet of the LORD with mockery aimed at the LORD's previous prophet is treated as covenant-violation. The bears are the covenantal sanction, not Elisha's revenge. (2) The northern kingdom's apostasy has produced a generation that mocks the LORD's prophets at the gate of Bethel — the very town named for the LORD's revelation to Jacob (Gen 28). The Bethel of revelation has become the Bethel of mockery. The covenantal curses are now operating on the northern kingdom precisely as Deuteronomy had warned. (3) The new prophet's authority — like Elijah's at Carmel and the bears like the fire from heaven against Ahaziah's captains — is being publicly verified. The mockery is answered with covenantal force. After this severe public event, Elisha 'went from thence to mount Carmel' — the site of Elijah's great victory, and probably a known prophetic retreat — 'and from thence he returned to Samaria' — the northern capital. The chapter is showing us Elisha entering the central public arena of Israelite politics with full prophetic credentials.

The curse is not Elisha's personal anger; it is invocation of covenantal sanctions (Lev 26 / Deut 28 had specified wild-beast attack as one of the covenantal curses on Israel's apostasy). The consequence: 'There came forth two she bears out of the wood, and tare forty and two children of them.' Two female bears (dubbim — bears, the feminine plural; she-bears are particularly dangerous because of cub-defense instinct) attack the group. 42 of the young men are 'torn' (Hebrew tibqa'na — split, ripped). The text does not specify deaths; the verb suggests serious mauling but not necessarily kills. The chapter is teaching us multiple hard lessons. (1) Mocking a true prophet of the LORD with mockery aimed at the LORD's previous prophet is treated as covenant-violation. The bears are the covenantal sanction, not Elisha's revenge. (2) The northern kingdom's apostasy has produced a generation that mocks the LORD's prophets at the gate of Bethel — the very town named for the LORD's revelation to Jacob (Gen 28). The Bethel of revelation has become the Bethel of mockery. The covenantal curses are now operating on the northern kingdom precisely as Deuteronomy had warned. (3) The new prophet's authority — like Elijah's at Carmel and the bears like the fire from heaven against Ahaziah's captains — is being publicly verified. The mockery is answered with covenantal force. After this severe public event, Elisha 'went from thence to mount Carmel' — the site of Elijah's great victory, and probably a known prophetic retreat — 'and from thence he returned to Samaria' — the northern capital. The chapter is showing us Elisha entering the central public arena of Israelite politics with full prophetic credentials.
